votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by computing the sum of the squares of the ceiling of each element and comparing it to the given sum.
The Python code correctly computes the sum of the squares of the ceiling of each element in the list, which exactly matches the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by computing the sum of the squares of the ceilings of the elements in the list and comparing it to the given sum.

The Coq spec says there exist integers zs such that each z is the ceiling of the corresponding x (i.e., z-1 < x <= z) and s is the sum of their squares. For any concrete real number x, math.ceil(x) is the unique integer satisfying this ceiling property, so the Python code computes the same sum and checks equality with s, matching the Coq spec on all concrete inputs.

The Coq spec says there exist integers zs such that each z is the ceiling of the corresponding x (i.e., z-1 < x <= z) and s is the sum of their squares. For any concrete real number x, math.ceil(x) is the unique integer satisfying this ceiling property, so the Python code computes the same unique sum that the Coq existential quantifier picks out. Both produce the same concrete result for all inputs.

The Coq spec defines is_ceil such that z is the ceiling of x (z-1 < x <= z), and requires s to be the sum of squares of these ceiling values. The Python code computes the same thing using math.ceil(x) and checks if s equals the sum of squared ceilings. For any concrete list of floats, both will agree on whether s is the correct sum of squared ceilings.
Both compute the sum of squares of ceilings of the list elements; math.ceil aligns with the Coq is_ceil characterization, and the summation matches the fold/map in the spec.
Both compute the sum of squares of elementwise ceilings of the input list; Python’s math.ceil matches the Coq is_ceil characterization, so results coincide on all real-number inputs.

Both compute the sum of squares of the ceilings of each list element. Coq’s is_ceil uniquely characterizes ceil(x) and sums z*z over the list; Python uses math.ceil(x)**2 and sums. They agree for all real-number inputs, including negatives and integers.
